title: Project progress summary with stories by status and verification percentage

story.i_want_to: query a project's progress summary showing story counts by agent_status and verified_status, epic completion ratios, and overall verification percentage
story.so_that: I can make informed decisions about what work remains, which epics are complete, and how far the project is from full verification

rationale: The progress summary is the primary dashboard endpoint for orchestrators. It provides a single-request overview of the entire project's state without needing to fetch and aggregate individual stories. The verification percentage (stories verified / total stories) is the key metric for determining project completion. Epic-level rollups help orchestrators prioritize which epic to focus on next.

acceptance_criteria.description: GET /api/v1/projects/:id/progress (role: agent+) returns a progress summary JSON object
The response includes stories_by_agent_status: {pending: N, assigned: N, implementing: N, reported_done: N}
The response includes stories_by_verified_status: {unverified: N, verified: N, rejected: N}
The response includes total_stories and total_epics counts
The response includes verification_percentage: a float (0.0 to 100.0) representing (verified stories / total stories * 100), rounded to 1 decimal
The response includes epics_completed: count of epics where ALL stories have verified_status = :verified
The response includes estimated_hours_total and estimated_hours_completed (sum of estimated_hours for verified stories)
The endpoint returns 404 if the project does not exist or belongs to another tenant
The query is efficient — uses aggregate SQL (GROUP BY, COUNT) rather than loading all stories into memory

test_cases.name: Progress summary with mixed statuses
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, agent_status: :pending, verified_status: :unverified})
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, agent_status: :implementing, verified_status: :unverified})
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, agent_status: :reported_done, verified_status: :verified, estimated_hours: 4})

test_cases.name: Progress summary counts completed epics correctly
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_a (all stories verified)
fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic_b (has unverified stories)
expected_results: epics_completed = 1 (only epic_a where all stories are verified)

test_cases.name: Progress summary scoped to current tenant
expected_results: Response status is 404 (cannot access another tenant's project)

test_cases.name: Progress summary for fully verified project
preconditions: fixture(:epic, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, project_id: project.id}) as epic
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, verified_status: :verified, estimated_hours: 3})
fixture(:story, %{tenant_id: tenant.id, epic_id: epic.id, verified_status: :verified, estimated_hours: 5})

test_cases.name: Progress summary returns 404 for nonexistent project

technical_notes: Use aggregate SQL queries for efficiency: SELECT agent_status, COUNT(*) FROM stories WHERE epic_id IN (SELECT id FROM epics WHERE project_id = ?) GROUP BY agent_status
Epic completion check: an epic is complete when it has at least one story AND all its stories have verified_status = :verified
Handle division by zero for verification_percentage when total_stories = 0 (return 0.0)
Consider caching this endpoint since it involves multiple aggregation queries — or use a single complex SQL query with CTEs

This story depends on US-6.2 (Story schema) being available, since it aggregates story data. If implementing before US-6.2, the context function can be written with the expected schema and tested once stories exist.
The Loopctl.Projects context should have a get_project_progress/1 function that returns a map of all progress data
All test modules MUST use `async: true` (e.g., `use Loopctl.DataCase, async: true` or `use LoopctlWeb.ConnCase, async: true`)
All tests using Mox MUST include `setup :verify_on_exit!`
All mocks MUST be defined centrally in `test/support/mocks.ex`, never in individual test files
